summary: Patrick McKenzie documents his painful experience implementing SSL encryption for his Rails application, prompted by the release of FireSheep, a Firefox addon that trivially compromises web session cookies on shared wireless networks. He walks through the technical steps: configuring Nginx for dual HTTP/HTTPS, scrubbing absolute URLs, using Rails plugins for SSL requirements, and dealing with IE-specific download bugs. The process caused significant downtime and he admits he should have used a staging environment first. He urges other Rails developers to test FireSheep against their own admin sessions and implement SSL immediately.
key_points: FireSheep demonstrated that any wireless user could trivially hijack web sessions, making SSL encryption urgent even for seemingly low-risk applications
Implementing SSL in Rails required coordinating Nginx configuration, URL scrubbing, Rails plugins, and browser-specific workarounds — far more painful than expected
Mixed content (HTTP resources on HTTPS pages) triggers browser security warnings, and each browser has different rules about which resource types require HTTPS
IE has unique architectural issues with downloadable files over SSL that require specific header workarounds
Even tamper-proof CookieStore sessions are vulnerable if the session cookie is ever transmitted over unencrypted HTTP
money_quotes: Implementing SSL in Rails was very painful and resulted in my site being down or unusable for a large portion of my customers for much of the day.

My theory was that my customer's didn't store anything security critical in their elementary school bingo card games, but the increasing amount of information available to the admin plus a customer support story from this morning convinced me that compromise would be a Very Bad Thing.

If I were doing it again, I would have paid the extra few bucks to set up a staging environment with its own certificate and verified everything worked on that prior to trying to fight my way through the process.
Do you think you're going to remember that? Yeah, me neither.
Note there is a subtlety here: if you let Rails share a session cookie over both HTTP and HTTPS, then if it is ever used over HTTP, byebye cookie security.
This option will probably break your site, possibly subtly, the first time you switch it on.
I suggest you download FireSheep and see how quickly any idiot with wireless can compromise your admin session. Then, fix this as soon as possible.
key_insight: SSL implementation is far more complex than flipping a switch — it requires coordinating server configuration, asset hosting, session security, and browser-specific workarounds, but the alternative is leaving every user session open to trivial hijacking.
